<!-- do not edit; create revised prose in manuscript-draft/ -->

## VII. The Shirt

It comes round by his housekeeper on the Tuesday: one shirt, good linen gone soft with decades of careful keeping, older than its owner's coats. The first washing Dr. Cray has sent the shop in fifteen years, arriving the week after his clasp came loose in front of her.

Her mother takes it in at the counter as though it were a shirt. Su washes it as though it were a shirt. It is not a shirt. It is a question, put the only way a careful man can put a question without ever having asked one: what does the laundry girl's face do, over my linen? She reads every seam of it as she reads knots, works the collar and cuffs through her fingers in the copper's steam, puts her face down into the wet cloth of it, as any laundress tests for what a stain has been — and there is nothing. Soap, and under the soap nothing, not tobacco, not sweat, not medicine: linen with nothing to say, which from that man is not innocence, it is grammar. She starves it of an answer. Boiled, pressed, folded, returned on the Friday with the ticket pinned where the shop always pins it, and her face, when the housekeeper collects it, is the shop's face and nothing else's.

Only afterwards, wringing sheets in the yard with more force than the sheets require, does she let herself understand what the week cost, and what it bought. He is watching her now. That is the shirt's plain meaning, and it cuts both ways — because a man who needs to know what a laundry girl saw is a man who has something a laundry girl could have seen.

* * *
